coredata.dc:title: Promoted peroxymonosulfate activation into ferrite sites over perovskite for sunset yellow degradation: Optimization parameters by response surface methodology
coredata.dc:creator: Tuna, Özlem
Bilgin Simsek, Esra
coredata.dc:description: The purpose of this research is to show the potential of CeFeO3 (CFO) perovskite as photocatalysts in PMS-activated AOPs for the degradation of target molecules. CeFeO3 photocatalyst was synthesized via a simple precipitation method. The structural, morphological and optical properties of the perovskite were characterized by XRD, SEM-EDS, and UV–vis diffuse reflectance analyses. Furthermore, the degradation efficiency of sunset yellow (SY) was investigated through batch experiments under different parameters (catalyst dosage, PMS concentration and initial dye concentration) and the results were optimized by applying Box-Behnken design method. The decomposition reaction was optimized in the presence of 0.49 g/L of catalyst dosage, 4.73 mM of PMS amount and 14.02 mg/L of dye concentration, with the degradation efficiency of 91.8%. It was observed that O2 •−, OH•, and SO4•– played dominant role in reaction. Furthermore, CFO particles were sufficiently incorporated with different carbon materials (graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), carbon fiber (CF) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)), showing excellent removal efficiency. This research shows inspiration for the treatment of polluted water with CeFeO3 under the synergistic effect of PMS and photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CeFeO3
Perovskite
Sunset
Photocatalyst
Peroxymonosulfate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental design modelling and response surface analysis
Radical identification and photocatalytic degradation mechanism
Incorporation of CeFeO3 particles with carbon structures
Practical applicability of CeFeO3 -deposited on different structured samples
Availability of data and materials materials-methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image and elemental mapping of CeFeO3.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) Tauc plot of CFO.
Photocatalytic degradation curves of CeFeO3 samples under PMS/Vis system.
3D plots of interactive effects between independent variables.
(a) Effect of trapping agents, (b) reaction constants and (c) possible degradation mechanism.
Recycle experiments and XRD XRD spectra of fresh and spent CeFeO3 photocatalyst.
UV–vis DRS spectra of (a), (b) Tauc plots, and (c) PL spectra of carbon-based the catalysts.
(a) Degradation efficiencies of different photocatalysts in PMS/Vis system, and (b) main degradation mechanism for carbon-incorporated samples.
(a) Degradation efficiencies of ZrO2-supported CFO samples and (b) polymeric filter-supported CFO samples in PMS/Vis system.
Levels and codes of different parameters.
Different parameters, observed and predicted removal performances.
ANOVA results for the dye removal.

head.ce:title: Promoted peroxymonosulfate activation into ferrite sites over perovskite for sunset yellow degradation: Optimization parameters by response surface methodology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Yalova University, 77100, Yalova, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Gebze Technical University, Kocaeli, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Gebze Technical University, Kocaeli, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Gebze Technical University, Kocaeli, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The purpose of this research is to show the potential of CeFeO3 (CFO) perovskite as photocatalysts in PMS-activated AOPs for the degradation of target molecules. CeFeO3 photocatalyst was synthesized via a simple precipitation method. The structural, morphological and optical properties of the perovskite were characterized by XRD, SEM-EDS, and UV–vis diffuse reflectance analyses. Furthermore, the degradation efficiency of sunset yellow (SY) was investigated through batch experiments under different parameters (catalyst dosage, PMS concentration and initial dye concentration) and the results were optimized by applying Box-Behnken design method. The decomposition reaction was optimized in the presence of 0.49 g/L of catalyst dosage, 4.73 mM of PMS amount and 14.02 mg/L of dye concentration, with the degradation efficiency of 91.8%. It was observed that O2 •−, OH•, and SO4•– played dominant role in reaction. Furthermore, CFO particles were sufficiently incorporated with different carbon materials (graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), carbon fiber (CF) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)), showing excellent removal efficiency. This research shows inspiration for the treatment of polluted water with CeFeO3 under the synergistic effect of PMS and photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, azo dyes have received an increasing concern for environment and human beings [1]. As an azo dye, sunset yellow (SY) has been presented as an important recalcitrant organic matter since it is utilized in many products, such as confectionery products, pharmaceuticals, and drinks. The sunset yellow contains phenyl and naphthyl rings bounded with azo functional group (NN), as well as sulfonate groups [2]. The structural properties negatively affect its removal by conventional methods. As a sufficient polluted water remediation process, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have shown great potential which have generally performed with different oxidants [3]. In this case, sulfate radicals-based advanced oxidation processes (SR-AOPs) have recently received a great deal of attention for the oxidation and mineralization of organic pollutants [4,5]. SR-AOPs are oxidative processes driven by the formation of SO4•- radicals from activation of persulfate anions including peroxymonosulfate (PMS, HSO5 −) and peroxydisulfate (PDS, S2O8 2−) [6]. To activate persulfate anions, several methods have been presented including the use of transition metals [7], light illumination [8], ultrasound [9], electrochemical process [10], microwave [11], and heat [6].

Transition metallic ions (Ag+, Co2+, Fe2+, and Mn2+) can be effectively applied in sulfate activated-AOPs. Among them, iron oxides have been used as heterogeneous catalyst owing to their high reducibility, versatility, easy accessibility, and eco-friendliness [12]. In this point, magnetic Fe3O4, an inverse spinel crystal, consisting of Fe(II) and Fe(III), have been commonly employed as PMS activator. Fe(II) species presented in the crystal lattice Fe3O4 play an important role in the activation system due to their high reduction potential. Meanwhile, Fe(III) sites can occur which can react with PMS, however the reaction rate is slow, resulting a relatively poor catalytic activity than Fe(II) [13]. Considering this fact, ferrite-type perovskites have attracted much attention in the PMS-assisted AOPs due to their abundant oxygen vacancies and unique oxidation state. Generally, spinel ferrite-type perovskites such as CoFe2O4 [14–16], MnFe2O4 [17,18], NiFe2O4 [14,19], CuFe2O4 [20,21] and ZnFe2O4 [22] are coupled with PMS systems due to their special superparamagnetic, optical and catalytical properties and high adsorption capacities [23]. The structure of MFe2O4 has a cubic phase packed with many holes where cations distribute at the tetrahedral and octahedral sites. The octahedral sites are generally found in the crystallites, and it is confirmed that these octahedral cations are responsible for the catalytic activity [23]. The reason is due to fact that the Fe cations placed in the octahedral sites (corner) provide effective interaction between Fe sites and PMS for sufficient degradation. It can be therefore stated that cubic and orthoferrite perovskites (AFeO3) in which Fe sites are more accessible show excellent photocatalytic activity under light irradiation in the presence of PMS [24]. In this point, LaFeO3, and BiFeO3 catalysts have been extensively studied to observe their photocatalytic effect on PMS-driven removal processes. For example, Rao et al. [25] showed that PMS-activated LaFeO3 decomposed antibiotic with a turnover frequency (2.02 × 10−3 min−1) which is 17-fold higher than that of Fe2O3. On the other hand. Han et al. [26] utilized BiFeO3 as an oxidizer of PMS to degrade significantly diclofenac. The efficient activity was attributed to more effective activation of PMS via the acceleration of Fe(III)– Fe(II)– Fe(III) redox cycle. Yao et al. [27] stated that antibiotic degradation efficiency of BiFeO3 enhanced via the addition of PMS in the reaction environment since the reaction between PMS and photogenerated holes promoted the generation of superoxide radicals. Due to the excellent potential activities, studies have been tended to new cubic-type perovskite as a candidate for persulfate driven photocatalytic decomposition reaction. In this point, cerium ferrite (CeFeO3), a rare-earth orthoferrite perovskite, has been newly gained attention because of its relatively narrow band gap energy (∼1.9 eV). The presence of abundant vacancies in the structure of CeFeO3 enable to alter the Fermi level to the conduction band under light illumination, thereby boosting its photocatalytic activity. Moreover, it has stable thermal and chemical features. Therefore, it is expected that the CeFeO3 perovskite has a potential to be utilized in PMS-activated AOPs, boosting the degradation of target molecules [28,29].

Herein, CeFeO3 photocatalyst was synthesized via a simple precipitation method for efficient degradation of sunset yellow (SY) in PMS-activated visible light system. The structural, morphological and optical properties of the perovskite were characterized by XRD, SEM-EDS, and UV–vis diffuse reflectance analyses. Furthermore, the degradation efficiency of SY was investigated through batch experiments under different parameters (catalyst dosage, PMS concentration and initial dye concentration) and the results were optimized by applying Box-Behnken design in response surface methodology. Besides, the reaction mechanism was examined by quenching tests and the reusability was examined in five-cycle experiment set. In addition, the incorporation of different carbon structures (graphitic carbon nitride, carbon fiber and N-doped carbon quantum dot) into CeFeO3 was studied. The effect of carbon-structure was also supported with PL and BET analyses. Finally, the effects of metal oxides (ZrO2 and Ce-doped ZrO2) and polymeric filters as support materials on the performance of CeFeO3 were investigated to show its applicability in long-term removal systems. To the best of our knowledge, this works presents for the first time the use of CeFeO3 as a photocatalyst for sunset yellow decomposition under Vis/PMS system, opening the door to new approach in the field of photocatalytic dye removal processes.

Cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) and iron(II) chloride (FeCl3·9H2O) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Ammonium solution (25%, NH4OH) were obtained from Merck. Potassium peroxymonosulfate (2KHSO5⋅KHSO4⋅K2SO4, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich) were applied as an oxidant agent in the decomposition tests. The chemicals used in the effect experiments (benzoquinone (≥98%, BQ), isopropanol (70%, IPA), ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid disodium salt (≥98.5%, EDTA-2Na)) were purchased from Merck. In case of the effect of the carbon incorporation in the perovskite, laboratory-in-made graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), carbon fiber (CF) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were used. Tetragonal ZrO2 and tetragonal Ce-doped ZrO2 were obtained from MEL, Chemicals. Polyester (PE) and polyurethane (PU) filters were purchased from KMC Filter Co., Ltd. Turkey. In addition, sunset yellow and tartrazine were utilized as organic pollutant in degradation experiments. All chemicals, except stated otherwise, were purchased commercially, and utilized without further purification.

A precipitation technique was performed to synthesize CeFeO3 sample. A uniform suspension was prepared by dispersion of 4.342 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 2.703 g of FeCl3·9H2O (99%) using 100 mL of distilled water. After stirring for 30 min, a certain volume of NH4OH (28%, v/v) was dropwise added into the solution when the pH achieved to 8. Then, the precipitated powder was filtered, and the calcination process of the resultant powder was conducted at 600 °C for 4 h in an air atmosphere. In addition, the as-obtained catalyst was named as CFO.

X-ray diffraction (XRD: Panalytical Empyrean) was performed to crystal phases examine of photocatalyst. It uses Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) operated with a scan range from 25° to 78° with 2°/min. The morphological structure of the photocatalyst was performed on a JEOL 6510 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy-disperse X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The surface of elemental property of the as-obtained perovskite was determined by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS: Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) with a monochromatized Al kα X-ray source and the relevant binding energies of the elements reference C 1s hydrocarbon peak at 284.8 eV. Ultraviolet visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were recorded from 200 to 800 nm on a PerkinElmer Lambda 750 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained at room temperature using a Photoluminescence Spectrometer equipped with PMT Detector 850 V, with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET:Oantachrome Autosorb IQ2) surface area analysis of the samples was performed by using N2 adsorption–desorption technique at −196 °C. The elemental analysis was also analyzed using X-ray fluorescence (XRF) spectroscopy.

The photocatalytic degradation performance of the as-synthesized catalyst was evaluated by the PMS-driven photodegradation of sunset yellow (SY: 10 mg/L) in a rectangular reactor including four visible lamps (Osram 150 W, λ: 400–800 nm) and two fans. Before light irradiation, the photocatalyst in dye solution was stirred under dark condition for 30 min to achieve the adsorption/desorption equilibrium. The aliquots were taken out at certain time intervals and filtered with 0.22 μm hydrophilic PVDF membrane filters (Merck). The liquid samples were analyzed using an UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer at wavelength of 482 nm. The mineralization of the dye solutions was determined via TOC values which were obtained from a Shimadzu TOC analyzer. The effects of trapping agents were performed by separately adding ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 1.0 mM), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 1.0 mM), ethanol (EtOH, 1 mM) and p-benzoquinone (BQ, 1.0 mM) into the reaction mixture. Moreover, to study the effect of carbon-structures on the efficiency of the perovskite the photodegradation of the azo dye was evaluated using heterostructured samples. To observe the practical applicability, CeFeO3-coated structured samples were also utilized in PMS/Vis removal process. The dye removal efficiency was estimated by following equation:
(1) Removal %= (C0–C)/C0 × 100%
where C0 and C are the initial and final concentrations at 0 and t min of irradiation time. In this study, the experiments were performed with triplicate independent, and the average values were showed as the relevant photocatalytic percentage.

Response surface methodology (RSM) combining with Box-Behnken design (BBD) was utilized to determine the influence of three different parameters on pseudo-first order rate constant of SY removal. The three different parameters are photocatalyst dosage (x1), PMS concentration (x2) and initial dye concentration (x3), respectively. The high, center, and low levels of each variable are denoted as +1, 0 and −1, respectively. Table 1 shows the levels of independent variables.

Regarding to the design data process, the variables were examined by a second-order polynomial with the three factors (Eq. (2)) [30]:
(2)
y =
b 0
+ ∑
b i
x i
+ ∑
b
i j
x i
x j
+ ∑
b
i i
x i
2
where y is the percentage of dye degradation; b0 is the intercept value; bi, bij and bii are linear, interaction and squared coefficients, respectively; xi, xij and xi 2 are linear, interaction and squared effects, respectively.

The XRD spectrum of CeFeO3 perovskite is presented in Fig. 1 . The pattern shows characteristic orthorhombic CeFeO3 reflection peaks detected at 2θ = 33.2°, 35.1°, 40.7°, 47.6°, 49.3°, 54.1°, 56.4°, 62.1°, 77.9° with crystalline planes of (1 1 2), (0 2 1), (2 0 2), (0 0 4), (2 2 1), (3 1 1), (0 2 4), (2 2 4) and (1 1 6), respectively (JCPDS Card No. 22–0166). In addition, two small diffraction peaks at 2θ = 64.2° and 70.2° were indexed to the CeO2 phase which was formed as impurity during synthesis of CeFeO3. In addition, the average crystallite size of CeFeO3 was calculated by using the Debye-Scherrer equation (Eq. (3)) [31]:

where D is assigned to crystallite size, λ presemts the wavelength of the incident X-ray (1.54 Å), β is attributed full width at half maxima (FWHM), and θ is diffraction angle corresponding to peak. According to the XRD spectrum, three intsense peaks were detected at 33.2°, 47.6° and 56.4°, which were attributed to (1 1 2), (0 0 4) and (2 2 4), respectively and the crystallite size was determined as 14.5 nm, 13.8 nm and 14.1 nm for the three angle values in same order. Thus, the average crystallite size was examined as 14.13 nm.

SEM technique is used to investigate the surface morphology of CeFeO3 as shown in Fig. 2 . The pristine CeFeO3 perovskite exhibited many spherical particles which were agglomerated that was attributed to the irregular structure. The elemental composition of the as-prepared catalyst was also detected using EDX analysis. As shown in Fig. 2, for the selected area, the perovskite was composed of cerium, iron and oxygen elements where they were homogenously dispersed over the surface according to the mapping analysis. Furthermore, the specific surface area and pore volume of CeFeO3 catalyst was obtained via BET and BJH methods. The BET specific surface area of the pristine perovskite was determined as 24.78 m2/g and the pore volume was presented as 0.162 cm3/g. In addition, XRF analysis was applied to examine the elemental composition of CFO: An elemental composition of 21.87 wt% O, 10.01 wt% Fe and 49.78 wt% Ce was observed in the sample. The results agreed well with the EDS results.

The UV/vis DRS was obtained in order to gain insight the photocatalytic performance of the CeFeO3 catalyst. It can be seen that the perovskite exhibited an absorption edge extended to visible range (Fig. 3 (a)). The band gap energy was determined by plotting parameters for Tauc expression ((αhⱱ)1/2 versus hⱱ) based on Kubelka-Munk function and the corresponding optical band gap was found at around 1.75 eV (Fig. 3(b)), indicating that the photocatalyst could be excited under visible light irradiation.

As a promising photocatalyst, cerium ferrite (CeFeO3), a rare-earth orthoferrite perovskite, was performed in the photocatalytically degradation of sunset yellow in the presence of sulfate-based oxidant. As shown in Fig. 4 , only 11.2% of SY was eliminated by CeFeO3 in the absence of the oxidant that was attributed to low separation rate of photogenerated carriers. When the persulfate ions were added in the reaction system, the removal efficiency significantly improved. The relevant degradation percentages were found as 67.5%, 84.9% and 95.0% in the presence of 1 mM, 3 mM and 5 mM PMS, respectively. Furthermore, Fig. 4 displayed the pseudo-first-order kinetic reaction plots of visible light driven SY degradation, and the reaction rate constant (k) was 0.0005, 0.0088, 0.0163 and 0.024 min−1 in CeFeO3/1 mM PMS, CeFeO3/3 mM PMS, and CeFeO3/5 mM PMS system, respectively. Considering that the activity of bare PMS could be negligible (only 12.6% degradation), it could be indicated that the role of PMS activation on the surface of CeFeO3 was more dominant than that of light irradiation. The enhanced activity of CeFeO3/PMS/Vis systems could be explained via different two main interactions of persulfate ions. PMS could be activated by visible light ann the ferrite sites into CFO could promote the activation of PMS. The interactions resulted in the generation of free radicals (SO4•- and HO•) which were responsible for the decomposition of the dye molecules. The overall activation reactions could be proposed as follows:
(4) HSO5 − + hv → SO4• + OH•
(5) HSO5 − + Fe(III) → SO5• + H+ + Fe(II)
(6a) HSO5 − + Fe(II) → SO4•+ OH• + Fe(III)

The TOC concentrations of initial dye solution and reaction solutions were determined to examine the mineralization ability of SY molecules in the presence of CeFeO3. An initial TOC value of 10 mg/L SY solution was found to be 9.789 mg/L, also after 120 min of light illumination, the TOC values were decreased to 8.735 and 3.259 mg/L for CFO/Vis system and CFO/PMS/Vis system, respectively. That is, in the visible light-driven catalytic removal process the total organic carbon removal achieved 10.77%, and 66.71% in the existence of PMS and the presence of PMS, respectively. According to the results, it could be stated that the TOC removal efficiencies were smaller than that of the decomposition, suggesting that the degradation of SY molecules into smaller molecules was easier than to mineralize it to CO2 and H2O.

In order to observe the applicability of the CFO/PMS/Vis system for sunset yellow removal, the efficiency of the system was compared with the other removal systems reported in literature. As shown in Table S1, it could be stated that the photo-persulfate catalytic system had greater activity than that of adsorption, extraction and catalytic ozonation processes. Although the photoelectron-Fenton and electrocoagulation systems provided more effective removal abilities, the removal processes required high operating costs [32]. On the other hand, the catalytic performance of CeFeO3 under visible light irradiation was compared with the other photocatalysts presented in literature and the relevant results was depicted in Table S2. From the results, it could be stated that the photocatalytic removal system showed greater performance than all other catalysts by using one-phase active material and minimum catalyst dosage. Thus, CeFeO3/PMS/Vis system could be classified in the group of efficient process for the removal of sunset yellow.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design modelling and response surface analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
A three factor Box-Behnken design consisting of 17 experimental runs was performed to optimize the degradation process and the relevant results are presented in Table 2 . Accordingly, the second order polynomial model was established as shown in Eq. (6).
(6b)
Y = 73.58 + 9.71
x 1
+ 12.72
x 2
− 7.03
x 3
− 1.12
x 1
x 1
− 2.96
x 1
x 3
+ 0.9693
x 2
x 3
− 3.97
x 1
2
+ 1.38
x 2
2
− 1.05
x 3
2

As indicated in the equation, the effects of catalyst dosage (x1) and PMS concentration (x2) on degradation rate constant of SY are positive, while the effect of initial dye concentration (x3) is negative. Moreover, the significance of variables on the SY removal performance is in the order of x2 > x1 > x3.

To determine the importance of the RSM model, ANOVA analysis was performed, and the relevant results are summarized in Table 3 . According to the table, the F-value of the model (52.59) was greater than 0.001, indicating the quadratic model was significant. Moreover, the coefficient (R2) of the quadratic model was 0.9854, indicating only 1.46% of the total variations could not be interpreted by the RSM model. Besides, the coefficient of adjusted determination (Adj R2) was 0.9667, which was close to R2, indicating the goodness of fit of the model. In addition, the adequate precision value (AP = 25.34) was much higher than 4, confirming the model was accurate in predicting the percentage of dye removal. On the other hand, Table 3 shows that the PMS concentration was the most important factor for the reaction since it had the highest F-value (238.92) while the values of the catalyst dosage and initial dye concentration were found as 139.02 and 72.87, respectively. The results were in a good agreement with the removal efficiency presented in the polynomial equation (Eq. (1)). It could be also stated that the mutual effects of the catalyst dosage and initial dye concentration was statistically important, resulting from the lower p values than 0.05.

The corresponding response surface plots are depicted in Fig. 5 . It could be seen that higher photocatalyst dosage and PMS concentration, as well as lower dye concentration favored the removal percentage of SY. The amounts of the oxidant and catalysts greatly enhanced the formation of radicals and the active surface sites, resulting increased decomposition of target dye molecules. However, in case of high dye concentration, the surface of the catalyst could be covered by the organic molecules, thus light excitation on the surface was limited, decreasing the formation of the photogenerated active species. Based on the RSM design, the optimum values were obtained as 0.49 g/L of catalyst dosage, 4.73 mM of PMS amount and 14.02 mg/L of dye concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radical identification and photocatalytic degradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Quenching experiments were carried out to identify reactive species involved in SY degradation in CeFeO3/PMS/Vis system. For this purpose, hydroxyl (OH•), hole (h+) and superoxide (O2•−) radicals were quenched by IPA, EDTA-2Na and BQ, respectively. EtOH was also used as trapping agents for both OH• (1.2–2.8 × 109 M−1 s−1) and SO4•– (1.6–7.7 × 107 M−1 s−1). The changes in SY degradation efficiency in the presence and in the absence of these scavengers are exhibited in Fig. 6 (a). 95.0% of SY was eliminated by CeFeO3/PMS/Vis without scavengers, while it decreased to 88.0%, 71.7%, 72.8% and 62.1% when EDTA-2Na, IPA, EtOH, and BQ were added into the reaction solution. Moreover, the reaction rate constants reflected the results: it was found as 0.0175, 0.0109,0.0112 and 0.0074min−1 in the presence of scavengers for h+, OH•, OH•/SO4•– and O2•−, respectively. These data depicted that the SY removal performance was markedly limited by the agents and suggest that all the aforementioned reactive species were responsible for the removal process. However, the strongest hampering effect of BQ implied that O2•− species showed the most dominant role than in the degradation mechanism of SY. In the presence of IPA, lower removal rate was observed than that of EtOH, indicating that OH• rather than SO4•– played a dominant role during the oxidation of the SY molecules. The experimental results of EDTA-2Na addition were in agreement with the previous literature that the photogenerated holes showed a slighter suppression effect on contaminant fragmentation in ferrite-type perovskite/PMS system [33]. Thus, O2•−, OH•, SO4•– and h+ had acted concurrently during the photocatalytic oxidation of SY by CeFeO3/PMS/Vis process.

A possible degradation mechanism for CeFeO3/PMS/Vis system was proposed in in Fig. 6 (c). Primarily, visible light photons were absorbed on the surface of CeFeO3, and subsequently the photogenerated electrons and holes occurred in the conduction and valence bands, respectively. Then, superoxide radicals formed via the adsorption of oxygen by photo-generated electrons in the conduction band. On the other side, the PMS was irradiated with the light, and SO4•/•OH species formed. The radicals also generated via the oxidation of the Fe3+ state in the perovskite, facilitating photocatalytic oxidation. The positive effect of the radicals.

The reusability of photocatalyst plays an important role on their ability to utilization in actual application area; hence CeFeO3 particles were carried out in SY degradation in Vis/PMS system for 5 cycles. Fig. 7 showed that the catalytic activity decreased by 8.1% after fifth consecutive experiments, indicating that the recycling property of the removal process was high. In order to check the stability of CFO in Vis/PMS system, the spent catalyst after fifth-run was analyzed by performing XRD analysis. As observed in Fig. 7, only minor changes were observed in the intensities of the main peaks, suggesting that the ferrite-type photocatalyst had structurally stable behavior. It could be concluded that CFO/Vis/PMS removal process was also of great interest in the decomposition of dye molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of CeFeO3 particles with carbon structures
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials have been considered as candidates in the formation of the heterostructure photocatalyst in AOP. Herein, to examine the effect of the carbon-introduction, the pristine CeFeO3 catalyst was combined with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), carbon fiber (CF) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs). For the heterostructured samples, a mixing-thermal method was applied: a specific amount of CeFeO3 was mixed with carbon-samples up to 5% of its weight in an aqueous medium at room temperature for 24 h, then the filtration and the drying processes were applied in order. The photocatalytic efficiencies of different carbon incorporated CeFeO3 catalysts were presented in Fig. 9(a).

According to the bare CFO (67.0%), the introduction of the carbon structures enhanced the degradation efficiency: CFO/NCQDs, CFO/g-C3N4 and CFO/CF showed 71.3%, 75.0% and 82.0% removal performance, respectively. Importantly, the dark efficiencies were calculated as 9%, 17% and %27 for CFO/NCQDs, CFO/g-C3N4 and CFO/CF, respectively whereas the pristine perovskite could adsorb only 5% of SY molecules. It could be showed that the efficiency of photocatalyst could increase by increasing adsorption ability, which could be attributed to high specific area. To support this phenomena, the specific area and pore volume of CFO/NCQDs, CFO/g-C3N4 and CFO/CF photocatalysts were examined by performing BET and BJH techniques. The BET specific area was determined as 3.21, 8.04 and 43.31 m2/g for CFO/NCQDs, CFO/g-C3N4 and CFO/CF respectively. Furthermore, when CFO was combined with NCQDs, g-C3N4 and CF, respectively, the pore volume was determined as 0.012, 0.093 and 0.100 cm3/g in order of the same manner. According to results, it could be stated that the type of carbon-structure played an important role on the specific area and pore volume in terms of CFO-based carbon heterostructure; thus, the photodegradation effenciceny significantly changed. On the other hand, UV–vis absorption spectra of the heterostructures (Fig. 8 (a)) were obtained to calculate the band gaps (Eg) of the heterostructures by by plotting electronic parameters for Tauc expression ((αhⱱ)1/2 versus hⱱ) based on Kubelka-Munk function. As seen in Fig. 8(b), the band gap values were determined as 2.75, 2.53 and 1.99 eV for CFO/NCQDs, CFO/g-C3N4 and CFO/CF respectively. Furthermore, to disclose the difference in the activity of the carbon modified samples, PL spectral analysis was carried out, which was seen form Fig. 8(c). It was known that high PL intensity was attributed to higher recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons and holes. According to the graph, the incorporation of carbon fiber into CFO sharply increased the photodegradation performances. It was concluded that the carbon-incorporated of CeFeO3 photocatalytic activity could be driven adsorptive and photoluminescence properties.

The addition of N-doped carbon quantum dot into CFO resulted in the formation of new active state, which could act as a trapping site for photo-generated electrons, demonstrating that the combination of the photoinduced charge carriers was efficiently inhibited, thus leading to improved photocatalytic activities [34]. After light irradiation, the active electrons and holes were formed in the conduction band and valence band of CFO, respectively and the NCQDs acted as electron acceptor, resulting the transfer of the photoinduced electrons to NCQDs. The photogenerated electrons in the conduction band of CFO and the accumulated electrons on NCQDs provided the formation of superoxide radicals. In further step, the dye molecules mainly oxidized into smaller molecules. Furthermore, via the addition of PMS, the active species (hydroxyl and sulfate radicals) were obtained by interacting PMS with the active electrons and ferrite-species in the catalysts, resulting further oxidation. On the other hand, N-included active sites of NCQDs could activate PMS molecules, increasing the formation of sulfate and hydroxyl radical from persulfate-based oxidant [35]. In addition, the photoinduced holes of CFO could promote the decomposition reaction. The mechanism was shown in Fig. 9.

The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CFO/g-C3N4 catalyst was predominantly attributed to the formation of heterojunction between the perovskite and graphitic carbon nitride [36]. The structure of interfacial junction between g-C3N4 and CFO could be proposed for general photoinduced carrier charge separation in which the photo-excited electrons on the CB potential of CFO recombined with holes located at the VB of g-C3N4 in the built-in electric field, prolonging the lifetime of the excitons. Meanwhile, the electrons on the CB of g-C3N4 were reacted with O2ads to generate superoxide radicals which decomposed the dye molecules. Then, sunset yellow was mainly oxidized to smaller molecules by the active species (Fig. 9). Besides, the accumulated electrons in the conduction band of the carbon nitride and the ferrite sites of the perovskite activated PMS to form hydroxyl and sulfate radicals, enhancing the decomposition of the organic molecules. The PMS could be also oxidized via light irradiation. In addition, the photo-generated holes on the VB of CeFeO3 provided the oxidization of SY. On the other hand, the structure of g-C3N4 contained amino groups (–NH2 or = NH) which provided the creation of the acidic property on the photocatalyst [37]. The effect of the property was reflected in increasing the adsorption rate of the dye molecules.

The positive effect of CF on the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CFO/CF depended on the strong electron conductivity ability of carbon fiber which inhibited the recombination of the photoinduced carriers. Moreover, due to the extended surface area of CF the adsorption capacity increased [38]. The reaction mechanism of CFO/CF was similar with CFO/NCQDs: after the formation of the photogenerated species in CFO via visible light illumination, the electrons transferred through carbon fiber site, increasing the separation of the photoactive phases. As main reductive species, the superoxide radicals were obtained via the interaction of oxygen molecules with the photoinduced electrons. The further radicals occurred the PMS activation via the electrons and ferrite species. In addition, the photogenerated holes played role on the reaction. The related mechanisms were summarized in Fig. 9. To conclude, the incorporation of the carbon-based structures into CeFeO3 greatly improved the photocatalytically degradation rate due to enhanced adsorption and photocatalysis performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applicability of CeFeO3 -deposited on different structured samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate practical applicability of CeFeO3/PMS/Vis system, the perovskite particles were immobilized different structured support materials; namely tetragonal ZrO2, tetragonal Ce-doped ZrO2, polyester (PE) and polyurethane (PU) filters. In the coating method, firstly, the pristine photocatalysts were dispersed into 20 mL ethanol solution under sonication for 30 min. The supported material was added into the solution and in the further step, the sonication was performed for 2 h. Then, the hydrothermal method was applied under 180 °C and 90 °C for metal oxides and polymeric-based substrates, respectively. The relevant photocatalytic activities were performed in PMS (5 mM)-visible light activated SY degradation systems and the results were presented in Fig. 10 (a) and (b).

It was clearly observed that the efficiency of all CeFeO3-deposited catalysts competed with the performance of the pristine perovskite (95.0%) in spite of smaller amount of active ferrite sites on the surface (38 wt% of bare CeFeO3). In terms of Zr-based supports, the photocatalytic activity was calculated as 62.4% and 67.4% for CFO/ZrO2 and CFO/Ce–ZrO2, respectively. The catalytic performances could be due to well-dispersed CeFeO3 particles on the surface of the metal oxide, resulting high more active surface area. The other reason could be stated that the deposition provided the isolation of the perovskite particles which inhibited the agglomeration of the active sites during the reaction. On the other hand, tetragonal ZrO2 involved limited hydroxyl groups, showing insignificant adsorption ability [39]. ZrO2 could be able to only UV wavelengths while ZrO2 could be sensitized to visible light via introduction in the material lattice of cerium ions [40]. According to the statements, herein the catalytic activities of the support materials were examined in the PMS/Vis system. The relevant removal percentages were calculated as 12% and 20% for ZrO2 and Ce–ZrO2, respectively and the results revelated good agreement with the degradation rates of CFO/ZrO2 and CFO/Ce–ZrO2.

In terms of polymeric filters, 78.5% and 83.2% of SY molecules were photocatalytically degraded in the presence of CFO/PU and CFO/PE. Besides, the pristine PE and PU materials had a notable adsorption affinity towards SY molecules which the removal efficiencies were calculated as 40.1% and 50.0%, respectively (Fig. 10(b)). The uptake mechanism of SY molecule over the polymeric substances was generally ascribed to the robust electrostatic interactions between sulphonic groups of SY and functional groups of polymers [41]. Therefore, the removal performance of CFO/PU and CFO/PE catalytic filters could be derived from the synergetic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis processes. In addition, the large specific surface areas of the polymeric templates might play an essential role which increased the interconnection of the dye molecules with the photocatalysts. The three-dimensional structure also provided significant transmission of CFO particles into the channels of the polymeric samples, improving dispersion ratio of the perovskite particles over the coated catalyst [42]. Furthermore, the porosity of the polymeric filters enhanced accessibility of catalytic sites to the light and the target molecules. It could be therefore stated that the deposition of CFO on the polymeric structures made the pollutant easier to adsorb and react, thus promoting the synergistic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis. To conclude, the observations demonstrated that the CeFeO3 perovskite particles were successfully dispersed on different metal oxide- and polymer-based supporting materials and the deposited substrates can be effectively used in practical removal applications.

In the present study, CeFeO3/PMS/Vis catalytic system was examined in dye removal process. The RSM/BBD model was used to optimize CeFeO3/PMS/Vis catalytic system for sunset yellow oxidation. In this model, the effects of photocatalyst dosage, initial dye concentration and PMS amount were studied. According to the design model, the removal efficiency improved photocatalyst dosage and PMS concentration, where high dye concentration limited the degradation performance. Based on the results of the ANOVA, it was determined that the model had good validity and the adequate precision value (AP = 25.34) was much higher than 4, proving that the mathematical model had good prediction accuracy. The trapping experiments depicted that O2•−, OH•, SO4•– and h+ had role during the photocatalytic decomposition reaction. Moreover, the incorporation of the carbon-based structures into CeFeO3 enhanced the catalytic removal efficiency due to the electron acceptor property of the carbon materials and enhanced active surface area. In addition, CFO particles was successful bonded on inorganic and organic substrates, allowing to synthesize structured photocatalysis systems for practical removal applications.
Not applicable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Availability of data and materials materials-methods
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
No ethical issues.
Consent.
Consent.

Özlem Tuna: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, and, contributed to the study conception and design. Material preparation, data collection and analysis were also performed by both the authors, The first draft of the manuscript was written by, and all authors commented on previous versions of the manuscript, All authors read and approved the final manuscript.“. Esra Bilgin Simsek: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, and, contributed to the study conception and design. Material preparation, data collection and analysis were also performed by both the authors, The first draft of the manuscript was written by, and all authors commented on previous versions of the manuscript, All authors read and approved the final manuscript.”.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gaggero E, Calza P, Cerrato E, Paganini MC. Cerium-, europium- and erbium-modified ZnO and ZrO2 for photocatalytic water treatment applications: A review. Catalysts 2021;11. https://doi.org/10.3390/catal11121520.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface modification of a polyethersulfone microfiltration membrane with graphene oxide for reactive dyes removal
Appl. Surf. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Homem NC, de Camargo Lima Beluci N, Amorim S, Reis R, Vieira AMS, Vieira MF, et al. Surface modification of a polyethersulfone microfiltration membrane with graphene oxide for reactive dyes removal. Appl Surf Sci 2019;486:499-507. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.04.276.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Deposition of CaFe2O4 and LaFeO3 perovskites on polyurethane filter: a new photocatalytic support for flowthrough degradation of tetracycline antibiotic
Environ. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tuna O, Karadirek S, Simsek EB. Deposition of CaFe2O4 and LaFeO3 perovskites on polyurethane filter: A new photocatalytic support for flowthrough degradation of tetracycline antibiotic. Environ Res 2022;205. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2021.112389.
